0.text: Olexa v Odon [2025] DIFC SCT 295 DECEMBER 10, 2025 SCT - JUDGMENTS AND ORDERS

1.text: THE DUBAI INTERNATIONAL FINANCIAL CENTRE COURTS

9.text: ORDER WITH REASONS OF H.E. JUSTICE SAPNA JHANGIANI

10.text: UPON reviewing the Judgment of SCT Judge Maitha AlShehhi (the “Judge”) dated 14 August 2025 (the “Judgment”)

11.text: AND UPON reviewing the Claimant’s Appeal Notice dated 1 September 2025 seeking permission to appeal the Judgment (the “Application for Permission to Appeal”)

12.text: AND UPON reviewing the Defendant’s response dated 2 October 2025 to the Application for Permission to Appeal

13.text: AND UPON considering the documents and submissions fi led by both parties and recorded on the case fi le

14.text: AND UPON hearing Counsel for the Claimant and Counsel for the Defendant at the hearing held on 28 October 2025 before H.E. Justice Sapna Jhangiani (the “Hearing”)

16.text: 1. The Permission to Appeal Application is refused.

17.text: 2. There shall be no order as to costs.

18.text:
Issued by:   
Delvin Sumo   
SCT Judge and Assistant Registrar   
Date of Issue: 10 December 2025   
At: 2pm

21.text: 1. The Claimant was employed by the Defendant from 6 December 2022 under a written employment agreement dated 8 December 2022 (the “Employment Contract”). The Claimant’s employment was terminated on 1...

22.text: 2. The Claimant fi led his Claim against the Defendant on 22 April 2025, more than 6 months after the termination of his employment. The Claimant’s outstanding Claim at the time of the Judgment was fo...

23.text: 3. The Claimant claims he is owed the Additional Salary Payments under the Release Agreement; alternatively, under a separate agreement entered into with the Defendant under which the Defendant offere...

24.text: a) The Defendant’s former CEO sent the Claimant the following message by WhatsApp on 8 May 2024 (the “8 May 2024 Message”):

25.text: “The offer of additional compensation has already expired, but I’m willing to extend it for this week as long as you’re responsive and sign before Friday”

26.text: b) The Claimant signed the Release Agreement on Friday 10 May.

27.text: 4. For completeness, it bears mentioning that the Claimant contends he had initially been offered six months’ salary as an incentive to resign by the Defendant’s former CEO during a meeting on 26 Apri...

28.text: 5. The Claimant claims that the Defendant’s promise through its former CEO that it would pay the Claimant the Additional Salary Payments if he resigned amounted to a clear promise that the Claimant re...

29.text: 6. The Claimant also sought an order imposing penalties on the Defendant of AED 300,162.86 pursuant to Article 19 of the DIFC Employment Law No. 2 of 2019 as amended (the “Employment Law”), due to the...

30.text: 7. The Defendant denies the Claimant’s Claim, submitting that the Claimant was paid all his entitlements in full under the Release Agreement, and that Schedule 1 to the Release Agreement sets out the ...

31.text: 8. The Defendant denies that it promised to pay the Claimant the Additional Salary Payments to resign, and contends that the doctrine of promissory estoppel does not apply. The Claimant’s breach of co...

33.text: 9. The terms of the Release Agreement are important to the determination of this case, and are set out below:

34.text: a) The recital provides that the Release Agreement “confi rms the circumstances surrounding the cessation of your employment with [the Defendant]”.

35.text: b) “Cessation Date” is defi ned as 10 June 2024 (unless the Claimant were to commence new employment before that date, which he did not).

36.text: c) Clause 4 of the Release Agreement is critical, and provides as follows:

37.text: “4. End of Service Payments & Final Settlement a. Odon will pay your fi nal settlement, which includes all your dues in respect of salary, leave and other benefi ts including end of service gratuity, ...

38.text: b. After the Cessation Date, Odon may elect, at its own discretion, to grant you some of the following additional benefi ts (“Additional Benefi ts”) for a limited period of 4(4) month(s): - monthly pa...

39.text: For the avoidance of doubt, all Additional Benefi ts granted in accordance with this Clause 4(a) shall immediately cease and terminate should you (i) begin a New Employment or (ii) be engaged as a ser...

40.text: By signing this Agreement, you hereby undertake to promptly, within fi ve (5) calendar days from signing an offer, agreement engagement letter, or similar documentation (including email acceptance) in...

41.text: c. You agree that the amounts contained in Schedule 1 of this Agreement, represents all your dues related to your end of service. By signing this Agreement you waive all your rights to claim any furth...

43.text: 10. The Defendant asserts that the Claimant’s Claim under the Release Agreement is time-barred pursuant to Article 10 of the Employment Law, which provides that this Court shall not consider a claim u...

44.text: 11. Article 20(2) addresses unlawful deductions made by an employer and Article 20(2)(a) provides that, in the case of a claim relating to a series of deductions from, or non-payment of, Remuneration ...

45.text: 12. The Claimant submits that the additional payments he was due under Clause 4(b) of the Release Agreement were a series of four payments under Article 20(2)(a)(iii), the fi rst payment of which shou...

46.text: 13. The Defendant denies that Article 20(2) is engaged because the Additional Salary Payments were never due to be paid by the Defendant.

49.text: a) The Claimant’s claim for penalties under Article 19 of the Employment Law could not be considered because the Claimant failed to quantify the penalty amounts in his Claim Form, and failed to fi le ...

50.text: b) There was “insuffi cient evidence to establish that the Claimant was forced to resign or that the additional payment was conditional upon signing the Release Agreement”. The learned Judge’s specifi...

51.text: c) The Defendant had no contractual obligation to pay the Additional Salary Payments under the Release Agreement as any additional payments were discretionary, as shown by the following wording in Cla...

52.text: d) In any event, the Claimant’s Claim was time-barred. The learned Judge rejected the Claimant’s argument that the Additional Salary Payments should have been been made on the 25th of each month after...

54.text: 15. The Claimant’s Grounds of Appeal are set out below in full, and shall be referred to as Grounds 1 to 6:

56.text: 1.1 At Paragraph 32 of the Judgment the Learned Judge found that because the Applicant failed to quantify the penalty sought in the Claim Form and pay an uplift in the Court fee, that he is not entitl...

57.text: 1.2 The Applicant submits that the Learned Judge erred in law and misapplied Article 19 of the DIFC Law 2/2019, as amended by DIFC Amendment 1/2024 (“DIFC Employment Law”). The penalty imposed on the ...

58.text: 1.3 It is not necessary for the Applicant to have calculated the penalty at the time of submitting the Claim Form (particularly as the Applicant fi led the Claim Form as a litigant in person and witho...

59.text: 1.4 The Applicant submits that an amendment to the Claim Form was not required, as stated by the Learned Judge during the hearing, as the Applicant was afforded an opportunity to fi le a further submi...

60.text: 1.5 Accordingly, the Learned Judge erred in law in dismissing this element of the claim. In turn, the claim became a civil contract claim and was no longer capable of being an employment claim (as fur...

61.text: Ground two: interpretation of the Release Agreement

62.text: 2.1 At Paragraphs 45 and 47 of the Judgment, the Learned Judge held the additional four months compensation to be paid under the Release Agreement are discretionary payments. The Applicant submits tha...

63.text: 2.2 The Learned Judge erred in her interpretation as she failed to interpret clause 4 as a whole and failed to consider the latter part of clause 4, concluding that the Respondent was entitled to use ...

64.text: 2.3 Accordingly, the four months additional compensation were already granted to the Applicant, and the Respondent was not entitled to use its discretion in the circumstances.

66.text: 3.1 Despite the Learned Judge’s dismissal of the penalty (as set out in ground one), the Learned Judge failed to recognise that the Release Agreement is a stand-alone settlement agreement, as acknowle...

67.text: 3.2 Accordingly, the Learned Judge erred in concluding that the claim does not fall within DIFC Contract Law which provides a limitation period of 6 years. The Applicant submits that this conclusion i...

69.text: 4.1 At Paragraphs 43, 44 and 48, the Learned Judge found that the claim was time barred, based on the Learned Judges error in interpreting the contract and incorrect application of the law as set out ...

70.text: 4.2 Had the Learned Judge held that that Release Agreement did in fact oblige the Respondent to make the additional four months compensation payments, time would have started on 11 October 2024. In ad...

71.text: 4.3 Accordingly, the Applicant’s claim was not time barred.

73.text: 5.1 Similarly, The Learned Judge was wrong in that she failed to consider or recognise the Parties’ promissory estoppel submissions. The Applicant submits that the Learned Judge was wrong not to have ...

75.text: 6.1 At Paragraph 46 of the Judgment, the Learned Judge was wrong in placing too much emphasis on the Applicant’s resignation. The Learned Judge failed to consider the Applicant’s evidence of fact whic...

76.text: 6.2 In addition, the Learned Judge failed to consider the facts presented that the Applicant had repeatedly sought the agreed payment from the Respondent and that the Respondent had never indicated th...

77.text: 6.3 The Applicant submits that the Learned Judge was wrong in that she failed to give appropriate weight to this evidence, the evidence being unchallenged by the Respondent.”

78.text: Applicable Principles for Permission to Appeal a Decision of the SCT

79.text: 16. Pursuant to Article 21 of DIFC Law No. (2) of 2025 (pertaining to the DIFC Courts), judgments of the Small Claims Tribunal may be appealed before the Court of First Instance where the appeal relat...

81.text: 17. Under Rule 53.87 of the Rules of the Dubai International Financial Centre Courts 2014 (the “RDC”), the “Court”, meaning the Court to which an appeal is made, will allow an appeal where the decisio...

82.text: (1) wrong (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings; or (3) wrong in relation to any other matter provided for or under any law.

83.text: 18. Under RDC 53.89, an application for permission to appeal a decision of the SCT must be made to the Court of First Instance in a particular form, and under RDC 53.91, permission to appeal may only ...

84.text: (1) the Court considers that the appeal would have a real prospect of success; or (2) there is some other compelling reason why the appeal should be heard.

85.text: 19. Applying the test that the appeal would have a real prospect of success requires a prospective assessment. In short, an applicant must show that there is a real (i.e. realistic as opposed to fanci...

87.text: 20. It is fair to say that the Claimant’s case was somewhat recast in the oral submissions advanced by the Claimant’s Counsel at the Hearing, as compared to the Claimant’s Grounds of Appeal and the Cl...

88.text: 21. As acknowledged by the Claimant’s Counsel in oral submissions, the central issue underlying the appeal is the learned Judge’s construction of the Release Agreement.

89.text: 22. The Claimant relied upon the canons of construction set out at Articles 49 to 55 of DIFC Law No. 6 of 2004, as amended (the “Contract Law”). In particular, the Claimant highlighted that, when cons...

90.text: 23. The crux of the dispute between the Claimant and Defendant is that the Claimant argues that the Release Agreement itself evidences that the Defendant had already exercised its discretion to grant ...

91.text: 24. A large part of the Claimant’s Counsel’s oral submissions focused on the “Good Leaver” / “Bad Leaver” provisions in the Claimant’s Employment Contract, which entitle a “Good Leaver” to three month...

92.text: 25. The Claimant argued that if the Claimant had been terminated for convenience, he would have received three months’ basic pay. However, he forewent that compensation when taking up the Defendant’s ...

93.text: 26. The Claimant’s primary case, as argued in oral submissions, was that he was entitled to three months’ mandatory base salary with an additional discretionary payment of a month; alternatively that ...

94.text: 27. The challenge with the Claimant’s case is that, even if his factual case is taken at its very highest, and an appeal court were to accept that the Claimant was induced to resign with the promise o...

95.text: a) The Claimant agreed that the amounts contained in Schedule 1 of the Release Agreement represented all dues related to the Claimant’s end of service and he waived his right to claim any further paym...

96.text: b) Clause 4(b) provides that after the Cessation Date, “the Defendant may elect, at its own discretion, to pay the Claimant a monthly salary for 4 months”. Even taking into account the conduct of the ...

97.text: 28. Based on the clear terms of the Release Agreement, I foresee no real prospect of success in an appeal in this matter on Ground 2 of the Grounds of Appeal.

98.text: 29. It is also clear that, according to the defi nition of a “Good Leaver” in the Claimant’s Employment Contract, he was not a Good Leaver. The Claimant’s argument in oral submissions that he “must ha...

99.text: 30. Given that Ground 2 of the Claimant’s Grounds of Appeal fails, Grounds 1, 3 and 4 fall away.

100.text: 31. I deal with Grounds 5 and 6 briefl y:

101.text: a) The learned Judge noted the Claimant’s promissory estoppel argument at [20] of the Judgment, and found that there was insuffi cient evidence to establish that the Additional Salary Payments were co...

102.text: b) As I have held above in relation to my construction of the Release Agreement, the aspects of documentary evidence relied upon by the Claimant under Ground 6 relating to pre-contractual negotiations...

103.text: i. I see no prospect of an appeal court interfering with the learned Judge’s fi ndings that that there was insuffi cient evidence to establish that the Claimant was forced to resign, notwithstanding t...

104.text: ii. The Claimant chased the Defendant for payment following the Release Agreement, and a number of WhatsApp messages were exchanged between the Claimant and the Defendant up to December 2024 referring...

105.text: Costs of the Application for Permission to Appeal

106.text: 32. Pursuant to RDC 53.118, I have a discretion as to the allocation of costs for the Claimant’s Application for Permission to Appeal. RDC 53.79 may be relevant as to how that discretion may be exerci...

108.text: The General Rule is that each party shall bear its own costs. However, the SCT may order a party to a small claim to pay a sum to another party in respect of that other party’s costs, fees and expense...

109.text: (1) such part of any Court or Tribunal fees paid by that other party as the SCT may consider appropriate;

110.text: (2) such further costs as the SCT may assess by the summary procedure and order to be paid by a party who has behaved unreasonably.”

111.text: 33. The Defendant submits that the Claimant should pay its costs of the Permission to Appeal Application on the basis that it was unreasonable for the Claimant to have pursued his arguments on appeal ...

112.text: 34. I decline to make an award of costs in the Defendant’s favour, and fi nd that each party shall bear its own costs of the Permission for Appeal Application. Whilst I have found that the terms of th...
